questions: Should the wallet registry (mapping wallet_key → private key env var) live in a DB table (`payment_wallets`) or in server_config JSONB? A DB table is more explicit and auditable; server_config is simpler but mixes payment secrets config with general settings.

For the Discord confirmation flow: should the service own the 'reply yes to confirm' interaction, or should it remain the producer's responsibility (i.e., GrantsCog collects the wallet and calls confirm_and_send)? The latter is simpler and lets each producer customize the UX.

What is the desired behavior for the test payment flow? Should it be mandatory for payments above a threshold, optional per-producer, or just available as a utility? This affects whether it's wired into the state machine or is a separate call.

Should the payment_ledger table live in the same Supabase project as grant_applications, or does it need its own security boundary (separate schema, separate service role)? The brief mentions 'strong table and secret security boundaries'.
For variable source wallets: is the initial scope just one wallet (grants_main) with the architecture supporting multiple, or are there already multiple wallets that need different keys today?

success_criteria.criterion: payment_ledger SQL migration creates a valid table with idempotency_key unique constraint, status CHECK constraint, and appropriate indexes
PaymentService.request_payment is idempotent — calling twice with the same idempotency_key returns the same payment_id without creating a duplicate
No double payments: if a payment is in 'sent' status with a tx_signature, confirm_and_send checks on-chain status instead of re-sending
State machine transitions are enforced — invalid transitions (e.g., completed → sending) are rejected at the DB layer
GrantsCog no longer imports or directly uses SolanaClient — all payment operations go through PaymentService
Startup recovery (recover_inflight) correctly handles payments in sending/sent status by checking on-chain and updating accordingly
All existing tests continue to pass after refactor
Unit tests cover: happy path, idempotency, double-send prevention, crash recovery, cancellation, invalid transitions
Provider interface is abstract enough that adding a token provider (e.g., USDC) requires only a new provider class, not service changes
Payment service file is under ~400 lines; provider files under ~150 lines each
wallet_key-based source wallet selection works — different producers can use different source wallets
SQL migration follows existing patterns: RLS enabled, updated_at trigger, timestamptz columns
Test payment flow (small amount validation send) is available as a utility method
Manual smoke test: approve a grant on devnet, confirm wallet, payment completes end-to-end through the new service

assumptions: The wallet registry will initially be env-var-based (wallet_key maps to an env var name containing the private key), not a DB table. This avoids storing private keys in the database and keeps the initial implementation simple. The architecture supports adding DB-backed wallet metadata later.

Discord confirmation flow remains the producer's responsibility. The payment service provides request_payment (creates pending entry) and confirm_and_send (executes after producer collects wallet). This keeps the service focused on payments and lets each producer own its UX.

The payment_ledger table lives in the same Supabase project and schema (public) as grant_applications. Security boundary is enforced via RLS and revoking anon/authenticated access, matching the social_publications pattern.
Only one source wallet exists today (grants_main → SOLANA_PRIVATE_KEY). The variable wallet_key architecture is built from day one but only one key is configured initially.
The test payment flow is an optional utility method, not a mandatory step in the state machine. Producers call it explicitly if they want pre-validation.
grant_applications table already exists in Supabase (created outside of checked-in migrations). We add a payment_id column to it but do not retroactively create a migration for the whole table.
The exchange rate is fetched at send time (not at request time) to minimize stale pricing, matching the current grants behavior.
